titleStmt.title: The Ambassador in Siam ( (Stanton) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Ambassador in Siam ( Stanton ) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 361. 1. Deptel 230, May 2,Not printed. received here May 5. Dr. Pham Ngoc Thach left Bangkok for Indo-China about 10 days ago. Colonel Law who saw him a number of times feels no doubt person visiting Bangkok and representing himself to be Thach was in fact Under Secretary of State Pham Ngoc Thach (Hanoi’s 136, April 24Not printed.). Specimen of Dr. Thach’s signature will be found on letter dated April 12 addressed to me, original of which forwarded to Department by courier with my despatch No. 363, April 23.Not printed, but see telegram 289, April 17, p. 87.

2. Referring information concerning Viet Nam internal situation, Colonel Law and myself propounded a number of questions and the following are Dr. Thach’s replies in summary: (a) Ho Chi Minh is in good health; (b) President Ho continues to direct the affairs of the Viet Nam Government and presides regularly over the weekly meetings of the Council of Ministers; (c) There have been number important changes in the composition of the Viet Nam Government since outbreak of present hostilities; (d) The Communist Party in Indo-China, according to Dr. Thach, is devoting its energies to fostering a triumph of the national cause and Communism for majority of members is merely a means of achieving independence. Text of questions and answers being forwarded by airmail both State and War Departments.Despatch 386, May 14, from Bangkok, not printed.

3. Fully appreciating delicacy of situation in Indo-China and that utmost discretion essential in dealings with Viet Nam officials or representatives, Embassy’s contacts with local representatives or visiting Viet Nam officials has been through Colonel Law, who some 6 months ago established contact with local representatives of Viet Nam, Free Laos and Free Cambodians. Through these contacts, which are entirely informal and personal, Law has been able to obtain much interesting information without attracting much attention, although these contacts are well known to local French Legation officials. Colonel Law has worked closely with me in these matters which we have discussed fully in every instance and I have utmost confidence in his discretion and ability. Upon Dr. Thach’s arrival he wished call on me, but it seemed to me, in view delicacy situation, much better for Law to make informal contact with him in view his previous contacts with Viet Nam representatives here.

4. Referring suggestion that O’Sullivan visit Bangkok on vacation, I would be happy to have him do so for although Thach no longer here principal Viet Nam representative here Tran Van Giau, alias Dr. Ouang, alias Nguyen Due Quon, might be useful contact. Dr. Ouang understood formerly to have been Ho Chi Minh’s chief lieutenant in Cochin China and recently attended New Delhi conference as the Viet Nam representative. However, wish to point out that arrival of O’Sullivan and any conversations he might have with Viet Nam representatives here would certainly come to attention French Legation officials who might attach undue significance such meetings.In telegram 45, May 9, 5 p.m., to Hanoi, the Department advised the Vice Consul that his proposed visit to Bangkok did not appear wise at that time (851G.00/5–747).
5. Department please repeat to Saigon and Hanoi.
